title: WebSockets in React Native Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of WebSocket connections, Socket.io, real-time patterns, and cleanup.

questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between HTTP and WebSocket communication?

options.text: HTTP is encrypted while WebSocket communicates in plaintext
WebSocket maintains a persistent bidirectional connection so both client and server can send messages at any time; HTTP requires a new request for each data exchange
HTTP supports multiple concurrent connections; WebSocket only allows one connection per app
WebSocket is built on UDP while HTTP uses TCP

questions.question: Why should you check `ws.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN` before calling `ws.send()`?

options.text: send() is 3x slower when the socket is not fully initialised
Calling send() on a closing or closed WebSocket throws an error; checking the state ensures you only send on an active connection
You must wait for OPEN state before listening for messages with onmessage
WebSocket.OPEN is a permission flag that must be set before the server accepts messages

questions.question: What does Socket.io's event acknowledgement pattern (the callback in `socket.emit()`) provide that vanilla WebSockets don't?

options.text: It provides message encryption for sensitive data
It provides guaranteed delivery confirmation — you know the server received and processed the event, not just that it was sent
It allows messages to be sent to multiple socket rooms simultaneously
It compresses the message payload to reduce bandwidth

questions.question: Why should you disconnect a WebSocket when the React Native app goes to the background?

options.text: iOS and Android forcefully kill WebSocket connections when apps are backgrounded
Backgrounded apps cannot receive WebSocket messages due to OS restrictions
An open WebSocket connection keeps the CPU active, consuming unnecessary battery; disconnecting and reconnecting on foreground is more power-efficient
The WebSocket server drops connections from backgrounded apps automatically

questions.question: What happens if you forget to close a WebSocket in a useEffect cleanup function?

options.text: Nothing — WebSockets automatically close when the component unmounts
A memory leak and ghost connection — the socket persists, continues receiving messages, and calling setState on the unmounted component causes errors
The component will be unable to re-mount until the connection times out
React Native crashes the app to prevent orphaned connections
